unit.name: Single-frame CNNs and late-fusion baselines
unit.description: Covers simple per-frame CNN baselines, frame sampling, and late-fusion designs that combine independently extracted frame features.
unit.summary: The first baseline treats video as a collection of images: sample frames, run a normal 2D CNN on each frame, and average predicted probabilities, which can work well when frames already reveal the action [ts=509s] [ts=557s]. The instructor stresses this is often a strong baseline and should be run first before building more complex video classifiers [ts=574s] [ts=585s]. Frame sampling is itself important: random sampling may work, but selecting informative frames or adapting the next sample based on earlier samples is an active research area [ts=618s] [ts=651s]. Late fusion improves on per-frame averaging by applying a 2D CNN independently to each key frame, producing T feature maps, then either flattening and concatenating them into a large vector for an MLP or pooling over space and time before a linear classifier [ts=692s] [ts=726s] [ts=819s]. The FC version can explode in parameters because the feature vector scales with T, while pooling keeps the feature dimension D but may discard important temporal information [ts=765s] [ts=841s] [ts=862s]. The central limitation of late fusion is that low-level motion cues, such as feet moving up and down, may be lost after many convolution and pooling layers before temporal comparison happens [ts=892s] [ts=960s].

key_points.text: A single-frame CNN runs an image classifier on sampled video frames and averages predictions.
Single-frame CNNs are often a very strong baseline and should be tried first when designing a video classifier.
Frame sampling is a key design question; random sampling is simple but not necessarily the smartest strategy.
Late fusion with FC layers concatenates independently extracted frame features into a large clip feature before an MLP.
Concatenation-based late fusion can introduce many parameters when T is large.
Pooling late fusion keeps feature dimension D but may remove information important for recognition.
Late fusion can miss low-level motion because high-level feature maps may no longer retain frame-to-frame movement cues.

kp_catalog.name: Single-frame CNN baseline
kp_catalog.description: A simple video classifier can sample frames, run a 2D CNN independently, and average probabilities.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because single-frame CNN baseline clarifies a simple video classifier can sample frames, run a 2D CNN independently, and average probabilities, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Late fusion
kp_catalog.description: Late fusion extracts high-level per-frame features independently and combines them near the classifier via concatenation or pooling.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because late fusion clarifies late fusion extracts high-level per-frame features independently and combines them near the classifier via concatenation or pooling, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
